name: Dazhbog
description: Dazhbog, also known as Dažbog, Dazhdbog, or Dadzbog, is a major solar deity in Slavic mythology. He is often depicted as the god of the sun, light, and giver of wealth and prosperity. His origins are shrouded in the mists of pre-Christian Slavic beliefs, but he appears in various historical chronicles and folklore. Dazhbog is seen as a cultural hero and a powerful force representing life and abundance. He is believed to journey across the sky each day in a chariot drawn by fiery horses, bringing light and warmth to the world. During the night, he travels through the underworld. He is also associated with agriculture, particularly the harvest, and is revered as a protector of the Slavic people. Some interpretations suggest he is a son of Svarog, the sky god, making him a grandson of Rod, the supreme creator deity. Dazhbog's worship was widespread throughout the Slavic lands, and he remains a significant figure in Slavic neopaganism and cultural identity. He symbolizes hope, vitality, and the enduring power of the sun. His role as a giver of wealth and prosperity makes him a benevolent and highly regarded figure in the Slavic pantheon. His name literally translates to "giving god" or "god who gives."
subtitle: Sun God, Giver of Wealth and Light

keyMyths.title: Dazhbog's Journey
keyMyths.description: Each day, Dazhbog rides his golden chariot across the sky, bringing sunlight to the world. At night, he travels through the underworld, preparing for his next journey.

keyMyths.title: Dazhbog and the Slavic People
keyMyths.description: Dazhbog is considered the patron deity of the Slavic people, offering them protection and prosperity. He is seen as a symbol of their cultural identity and resilience.

keyMyths.title: Dazhbog's Lineage
keyMyths.description: Some accounts depict Dazhbog as the son of Svarog, the sky god, and a grandson of Rod, the supreme creator. This lineage reinforces his position as a powerful and important deity.

sources.author: Primary Chronicle
sources.description: An early East Slavic chronicle that mentions Dazhbog.
sources.text: An early East Slavic chronicle that mentions Dazhbog.

sources.author: The Tale of Igor's Campaign
sources.description: A 12th-century epic poem that references Dazhbog as a symbol of Slavic identity.
